title: Vegetarian Wellington
subtitle: Mushroom, chestnut, and walnut filling wrapped in puff pastry, a show-stopping centrepiece
excerpt: A vegetarian wellington is a firm, flavourful log of mushroom duxelles, chestnuts, walnuts, and cream cheese wrapped in puff pastry and baked until deeply golden. The key is drying the filling completely before wrapping: any moisture will make the pastry soggy from inside.
sourceNotes: Contemporary British vegetarian cooking. Adapted from the beef Wellington tradition (19th century). Yotam Ottolenghi and the broader vegetarian centrepiece cooking tradition in British food from the 2010s onwards.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the assembled but unbaked wellington for up to 1 month. Bake from frozen at 200°C for 55-60 minutes, tenting loosely with foil if the pastry colours too quickly.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The filling can be made a day ahead and refrigerated. Assemble and wrap on the day, chill for 30 minutes, then bake.

glossaryTerms.definition: A wellington filling must be firm enough to slice cleanly without collapsing once the pastry is cut. For vegetarian versions, this means cooking off all excess moisture from the mushrooms and spinach before the other ingredients are added, and allowing the cream cheese and nuts to bind the mixture as it cools. The filling should be cool and firm when the pastry goes on.

Puff pastry seals best when pressed firmly together without stretching. Stretched seams split during baking as the pastry puffs. Overlap the pastry generously on the underside and press to seal; dampen the edges with a little cold water to help adhesion. Score the top without cutting all the way through.

content.text: The central challenge of a vegetarian

is moisture. Mushrooms and spinach both contain large amounts of water that must be cooked out before they are combined with the other ingredients. If they go into the pastry wet, the moisture escapes during baking and the base turns soft.
The filling must be cold before the pastry goes on. Warm filling makes the pastry greasy and prevents the sharp base crust forming. Refrigerate it for at least 30 minutes after the
in a large frying pan over medium-high heat. Add the onion and cook for 5 minutes. Add garlic, mushroom stalks, and thyme and cook for 3 minutes. Add the
and cook until completely wilted. Transfer to a sieve and press out all liquid. Return to the pan and cook, stirring, until the pan is dry. Season with
. Transfer to a bowl. Fold in the
. Taste and adjust seasoning. Cool completely, then refrigerate until cold.
Heat the oven to 200°C (180°C fan). On a lightly floured surface, roll out the
to a rectangle large enough to enclose the filling. Sear the whole portobello mushrooms in a hot, dry pan for 2 minutes per side. Brush the pastry with

. Arrange the portobello mushrooms in a row down the centre. Spoon the cold filling over the mushrooms and shape into a firm log. Fold the pastry over the top, press the edges firmly to seal, and crimp. Place seam-side down on a lined baking tray. Score the top and brush with beaten egg. Refrigerate for 30 minutes.
Brush with a second coat of beaten egg. Bake for 35-40 minutes until the pastry is deep golden all over. Rest for 10 minutes before slicing with a serrated knife.

rows.cause: Filling was too wet, or wellington not rested before cutting
rows.fix: The filling must be pressed completely dry and refrigerated cold before wrapping. Bake on a preheated tray to give the base a head start.

rows.symptom: Pastry splits along the seam during baking
rows.cause: Seam was stretched rather than pressed
rows.fix: Press the seam firmly using the heel of your hand, not a crimp. Ensure the pastry is not stretched when folded.

rows.cause: Not chilled long enough before baking, or filling was too soft
rows.fix: 30 minutes of chilling is the minimum. The cream cheese should firm as it cools; if the filling feels too loose, fold in an extra 50g of breadcrumbs.

rows.cause: Oven not hot enough, or egg wash not applied
rows.fix: Puff pastry needs 200°C and two coats of egg wash. Allow the first coat to dry for 5 minutes before the second.

content.text: The vegetarian wellington emerged from the same impulse that produced the nut roast: a centrepiece dish for vegetarians at British Christmas and Sunday tables that could hold its own against the traditional roast. In its best versions it does rather more than that, producing a dish with a depth of flavour from the mushrooms and chestnuts that the nut roast rarely achieves. Serve with roast potatoes, gravy, and green vegetables.
